metadata.title: Scientific Considerations in Demonstrating Biosimilarity to a Reference Product: Guidance for Industry

rules.rule_text: A 351(k) application must contain information demonstrating biosimilarity based on data derived from analytical studies, animal studies, and clinical studies, unless FDA determines that an element is unnecessary.
rules.verbatim_quote: An application submitted under section 351(k) of the PHS Act must contain, among other things, information demonstrating that 'the biological product is biosimilar to a reference product' based upon data derived from: Analytical studies that demonstrate that the biological product is highly similar to the reference product notwithstanding minor differences in clinically inactive components; Animal studies (including the assessment of toxicity); and A clinical study or studies (including the assessment of immunogenicity and pharmacokinetics or pharmacodynamics).
rules.exceptions: The Agency has the discretion to determine that an element described above is unnecessary in a 351(k) application.
cross_references: Section 7002(a)(2) of the Affordable Care Act

rules.rule_text: To obtain licensure under section 351(k), a sponsor must demonstrate that the proposed product is biosimilar to a single reference product that has been previously licensed by FDA.
rules.source_section: Section V, U.S.-Licensed Reference Product and Other Comparators
rules.verbatim_quote: To obtain licensure of a proposed product under section 351(k) of the PHS Act, a sponsor must demonstrate that the proposed product is biosimilar to a single reference product that previously has been licensed by FDA.
rules.exceptions: A sponsor may seek to use data derived from animal or clinical studies comparing a proposed product with a non-U.S.-licensed comparator product, with adequate scientific justification and an acceptable bridge to the U.S.-licensed reference product.
cross_references: Sections 7002(a)(2) and (b)(3) of the Affordable Care Act

rules.rule_text: Analytical studies and at least one clinical PK study and, if appropriate, at least one PD study intended to support a demonstration of biosimilarity must include an adequate comparison of the proposed biosimilar product directly with the U.S.-licensed reference product.
rules.source_section: Section V, U.S.-Licensed Reference Product and Other Comparators
rules.verbatim_quote: As a scientific matter, analytical studies and at least one clinical PK study and, if appropriate, at least one PD study, intended to support a demonstration of biosimilarity for purposes of section 351(k) of the PHS Act must include an adequate comparison of the proposed biosimilar product directly with the U.S.-licensed reference product unless it can be scientifically justified that such a study is not needed.
rules.exceptions: Can be scientifically justified that such a study is not needed.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must use a stepwise approach to develop the data and information needed to support a demonstration of biosimilarity, starting with extensive structural and functional characterization.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A, Using a Stepwise Approach to Demonstrate Biosimilarity
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA recommends that sponsors use a stepwise approach to developing the data and information needed to support a demonstration of biosimilarity. At each step, the sponsor should evaluate the extent to which there is residual uncertainty about the biosimilarity of the proposed product and identify next steps to try to address that uncertainty.

rules.rule_text: FDA will consider the totality of the evidence provided by a sponsor when evaluating the sponsor's demonstration of biosimilarity, including structural and functional characterization, nonclinical evaluation, human PK and PD data, clinical immunogenicity data, and comparative clinical study data.
rules.source_section: Section VI.B, Using a Totality-of-the-Evidence Approach to Assess a Demonstration of Biosimilarity
rules.verbatim_quote: In evaluating a sponsor's demonstration of biosimilarity, FDA will consider the totality of the data and information submitted in the application, including structural and functional characterization, nonclinical evaluation, human PK and PD data, clinical immunogenicity data, and comparative clinical study(ies) data. FDA intends to use a risk-based approach to evaluate all available data and information submitted in support of the biosimilarity of the proposed product.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must extensively characterize the proposed product and the reference product with state-of-the-art technology; the expression construct for a proposed product is expected to encode the same primary amino acid sequence as its reference product.
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA expects that first, a sponsor will extensively characterize the proposed product and the reference product with state-of-the-art technology, because extensive characterization of both products serves as the foundation for a demonstration of biosimilarity. It is expected that the expression construct for a proposed product will encode the same primary amino acid sequence as its reference product.
rules.exceptions: Minor modifications such as N- or C-terminal truncations that are not expected to change the product performance may be justified and should be explained by the sponsor.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must consider all relevant characteristics of the protein product in structural analyses, including primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure; posttranslational modifications; and biological activities.
rules.verbatim_quote: Additionally, sponsors should consider all relevant characteristics of the protein product (e.g., the primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure; posttranslational modifications; and biological activities) to demonstrate that the proposed product is highly similar to the reference product notwithstanding minor differences in clinically inactive components.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must conduct extensive structural characterization of both the proposed product and the reference product in multiple representative lots to understand lot-to-lot variability, with lot selection justified including the number of lots.
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should conduct extensive structural characterization of both the proposed product and the reference product in multiple representative lots to understand the lot-to-lot variability of both products in the manufacturing processes. ... Sponsors should justify the selection of the representative lots, including the number of lots.

rules.rule_text: FDA recommends that sponsors analyze the finished dosage form of multiple lots of both the proposed product and the reference product, assessing excipients and any formulation effect on purity, product- and process-related impurities, and stability.
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition, FDA recommends that sponsors analyze the finished dosage form of multiple lots of the proposed product and the reference product, assessing excipients and any formulation effect on purity, product- and process-related impurities, and stability.
cross_references: Quality Considerations in Demonstrating Biosimilarity of a Therapeutic Protein Product to a Reference Product

rules.rule_text: If the reference product or the proposed product cannot be adequately characterized with state-of-the-art technology, the application for the proposed product may not be appropriate for submission under section 351(k).
rules.verbatim_quote: If the reference product or the proposed product cannot be adequately characterized with state-of-the-art technology, the application for the proposed product may not be appropriate for submission under section 351(k) of the PHS Act; and the sponsor should consult FDA for guidance on the appropriate submission pathway.

rules.rule_text: Functional assays must be comparative so they can provide evidence of similarity or reveal differences in performance between the proposed product and the reference product; FDA recommends that sponsors discuss limitations of the assays in their submissions.
rules.verbatim_quote: These assays are expected to be comparative so they can provide evidence of similarity or reveal differences in the performance of the proposed product compared to the reference product, especially differences resulting from variations in structure that cannot be detected using current analytical methods. FDA also recommends that sponsors discuss limitations of the assays they used when interpreting results in their submissions to FDA.

rules.rule_text: The PHS Act requires that a 351(k) application include information demonstrating biosimilarity based on data from animal studies (including the assessment of toxicity), unless FDA determines that such studies are not necessary.
rules.verbatim_quote: The PHS Act also requires that a 351(k) application include information demonstrating biosimilarity based on data derived from animal studies (including the assessment of toxicity), unless FDA determines that such studies are not necessary in a 351(k) application.
rules.exceptions: FDA determines that such studies are not necessary in a 351(k) application. Also: Safety data from animal toxicity studies generally are not expected if clinical data using the proposed product are available (with the same proposed route of administration and formulation) that provide sufficient evidence for its safe use.

rules.rule_text: Animal toxicity studies are generally not useful if there is no animal species that can provide pharmacologically relevant data for the product; additional comparative in vitro testing using human cells or tissues is encouraged in such cases.
rules.verbatim_quote: Animal toxicity studies are generally not useful if there is no animal species that can provide pharmacologically relevant data for the product (i.e., no species in which the biologic activity of the product mimics the human response). ... If animal toxicity studies are not warranted based on an acceptable scientific justification, additional comparative in vitro testing (using human cells or tissues when appropriate) is encouraged.

rules.rule_text: In general, nonclinical safety pharmacology, reproductive and developmental toxicity, and carcinogenicity studies are not warranted when the proposed product and the reference product have been demonstrated to be highly similar through extensive structural and functional characterization and animal toxicity studies.
rules.verbatim_quote: In general, nonclinical safety pharmacology, reproductive and developmental toxicity, and carcinogenicity studies are not warranted when the proposed product and the reference product have been demonstrated to be highly similar through extensive structural and functional characterization and animal toxicity studies (if such studies were conducted).

rules.rule_text: Animal PK and PD assessment will not negate the need for human PK and PD studies.
rules.source_section: Section VII.C.2, Inclusion of Animal PK and PD Measures
rules.verbatim_quote: Animal PK and PD assessment will not negate the need for human PK and PD studies.

rules.rule_text: FDA expects a sponsor to conduct comparative human PK and PD studies (if there is a relevant PD measure) and a clinical immunogenicity assessment as fundamental components of supporting a demonstration of biosimilarity.
rules.source_section: Section VII.D, Clinical Studies - General Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: As a scientific matter, FDA expects a sponsor to conduct comparative human PK and PD studies (if there is a relevant PD measure(s)) and a clinical immunogenicity assessment. In certain cases, the results of these studies may provide adequate clinical data to support a conclusion that there are no clinically meaningful differences between the proposed biosimilar product and the reference product.
rules.exceptions: Sponsor can scientifically justify that a PK or PD study is not needed.

rules.rule_text: Human PK and PD studies comparing a proposed product to the reference product are generally fundamental components for supporting a demonstration of biosimilarity; both PK and PD studies will generally be expected to establish biosimilarity.
rules.verbatim_quote: Human PK and PD profiles of a protein product often cannot be adequately predicted from functional assays and/or animal studies alone. Therefore, human PK and PD studies comparing a proposed product to the reference product generally are fundamental components in supporting a demonstration of biosimilarity. Both PK and PD studies (where there is a relevant PD measure(s)) generally will be expected to establish biosimilarity, unless a sponsor can scientifically justify that such a study is not needed.
rules.exceptions: Sponsor can scientifically justify that such a study is not needed.

rules.rule_text: Comparative human PK and PD studies should use a population, dose(s), and route of administration that are adequately sensitive to allow detection of differences in PK and PD profiles.
rules.verbatim_quote: For example, comparative human PK and PD studies should use a population, dose(s), and route of administration that are adequately sensitive to allow for the detection of differences in PK and PD profiles.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must predefine and justify the criteria for PK and PD parameters for studies included in the application to demonstrate biosimilarity.
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should predefine and justify the criteria for PK and PD parameters for studies included in the application to demonstrate biosimilarity.

rules.rule_text: For PD studies using products with a short half-life (shorter than 5 days), a rapid PD response, and a low incidence of immunogenicity, a crossover design is appropriate when feasible; for products with a longer half-life (more than 5 days), a parallel design will usually be needed.
rules.verbatim_quote: For PD studies using products with a short half-life (e.g., shorter than 5 days), a rapid PD response, and a low incidence of immunogenicity, a crossover design is appropriate, when feasible. For products with a longer half-life (e.g., more than 5 days), a parallel design will usually be needed.

rules.rule_text: At least one clinical study that includes a comparison of the immunogenicity of the proposed product to that of the reference product will be expected; FDA encourages collection of immunogenicity data in any clinical study including human PK or PD studies.
rules.verbatim_quote: Structural, functional, and animal data are generally not adequate to predict immunogenicity in humans. Therefore, at least one clinical study that includes a comparison of the immunogenicity of the proposed product to that of the reference product will be expected. FDA encourages that, where feasible, sponsors collect immunogenicity data in any clinical study, including human PK or PD studies.

rules.rule_text: FDA recommends use of a comparative parallel design (head-to-head study) in treatment-naive patients as the most sensitive design for a premarketing study to assess potential differences in the risk of immunogenicity.
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA recommends use of a comparative parallel design (i.e., a head-to-head study) in treatment-naïve patients as the most sensitive design for a premarketing study to assess potential differences in the risk of immunogenicity.
rules.exceptions: A sponsor may need to evaluate a subset of patients to provide a substantive descriptive assessment of whether a single cross-over from the reference product to the proposed biosimilar would result in a major risk.

rules.rule_text: The design of any study to assess immunogenicity and acceptable differences in the incidence and other parameters of immune response must be discussed with FDA before initiating the study.
rules.verbatim_quote: The design of any study to assess immunogenicity and acceptable differences in the incidence and other parameters of immune response should be discussed with FDA before initiating the study.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must prospectively define the clinical immune response criteria for each type of potential immune response and obtain agreement from FDA on these criteria before initiating the study.
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should prospectively define the clinical immune response criteria (e.g., definitions of significant clinical events such as anaphylaxis), using established criteria where available, for each type of potential immune response and should obtain agreement from FDA on these criteria before initiating the study.

rules.rule_text: For chronically administered agents, the follow-up period for immunogenicity assessment is recommended to be 1 year unless a shorter duration can be scientifically justified based on the totality of the evidence to support biosimilarity.
rules.verbatim_quote: For example, for chronically administered agents, the follow-up period is recommended to be 1 year unless a shorter duration can be scientifically justified based on the totality of the evidence to support biosimilarity.
rules.exceptions: Shorter duration can be scientifically justified based on the totality of the evidence to support biosimilarity.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must develop assays capable of sensitively detecting immune responses even in the presence of the circulating drug product; the proposed product and the reference product must be assessed in the same assay with the same patient sera whenever possible.
rules.verbatim_quote: The sponsor should develop assays capable of sensitively detecting immune responses, even in the presence of the circulating drug product (proposed product and reference product). The proposed product and the reference product should be assessed in the same assay with the same patient sera whenever possible.

rules.rule_text: Immunogenicity assays must be developed and validated early in development; sponsors must consult with FDA on the sufficiency of assays before initiating any clinical immunogenicity assessment.
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA recommends that immunogenicity assays be developed and validated early in development, and the validation should consider both the proposed product and the reference product. Sponsors should consult with FDA on the sufficiency of assays before initiating any clinical immunogenicity assessment.

rules.rule_text: A comparative clinical study will be necessary when there is residual uncertainty about whether there are clinically meaningful differences between the proposed product and the reference product after structural and functional characterization, animal testing, human PK and PD data, and clinical immunogenicity assessment.
rules.verbatim_quote: As a scientific matter, a comparative clinical study will be necessary to support a demonstration of biosimilarity if there is residual uncertainty about whether there are clinically meaningful differences between the proposed product and the reference product based on structural and functional characterization, animal testing, human PK and PD data, and clinical immunogenicity assessment. A sponsor should provide a scientific justification if it believes that a comparative clinical study is not necessary.
rules.exceptions: Sponsor should provide a scientific justification if it believes that a comparative clinical study is not necessary.

rules.rule_text: A comparative clinical study for a biosimilar development program should be designed to investigate whether there are clinically meaningful differences between the proposed product and the reference product; generally FDA expects an equivalence design.
rules.source_section: Section VII.D.3, Comparative Clinical Studies - Study Design and Analyses
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally, FDA expects a clinical study or studies designed to establish statistical evidence that the proposed product is neither inferior to the reference product by more than a specified margin nor superior to the reference product by more than a (possibly different) specified margin. Typically, an equivalence design with symmetric inferiority and superiority margins would be used.
rules.exceptions: An asymmetric interval or a non-inferiority only design may be appropriate under specific scientifically justified circumstances.

rules.rule_text: A sponsor must provide adequate scientific justification for the choice of study design, study population, study endpoint(s), estimated effect size for the reference product, and margin(s); sponsors must discuss their study proposals with FDA before initiating the comparative clinical study.
rules.source_section: Section VII.D.3, Comparative Clinical Studies - Study Design and Analyses
rules.verbatim_quote: A sponsor should provide adequate scientific justification for the choice of study design, study population, study endpoint(s), estimated effect size for the reference product, and margin(s) (how much difference to rule out). Sponsors should discuss their study proposal(s) and overall clinical development plan with FDA before initiating the comparative clinical study(ies).

rules.rule_text: For extrapolation of clinical data to additional conditions of use, the applicant must provide sufficient scientific justification for each condition of use for which licensure is sought.
rules.source_section: Section VII.D.4, Extrapolation of Clinical Data Across Indications
rules.verbatim_quote: However, the applicant would need to provide sufficient scientific justification for extrapolating clinical data to support a determination of biosimilarity for each condition of use for which licensure is sought.

rules.rule_text: Scientific justification for extrapolation must address the mechanism(s) of action, PK and bio-distribution, immunogenicity, and differences in expected toxicities in each condition of use and patient population.
rules.source_section: Section VII.D.4, Extrapolation of Clinical Data Across Indications
rules.verbatim_quote: Such scientific justification for extrapolation should address, for example, the following issues for the tested and extrapolated conditions of use: The MOA(s) in each condition of use for which licensure is sought... The PK and bio-distribution of the product in different patient populations... The immunogenicity of the product in different patient populations... Differences in expected toxicities in each condition of use and patient population.
rules.exceptions: Differences between conditions of use do not necessarily preclude extrapolation; a scientific justification should address differences in the context of the totality of the evidence.

rules.rule_text: A proposed product may only obtain licensure for a condition of use that has been previously licensed for the reference product.
rules.source_section: Section VII.D.4, Extrapolation of Clinical Data Across Indications
rules.verbatim_quote: The sponsor of a proposed product may obtain licensure only for a condition of use that has been previously licensed for the reference product.

rules.rule_text: Robust postmarketing safety monitoring is required for biosimilar therapeutic protein products and must have adequate mechanisms to differentiate between adverse events associated with the proposed product and those associated with the reference product.
rules.source_section: Section VIII, Postmarketing Safety Monitoring Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Postmarketing safety monitoring for a proposed product should also have adequate mechanisms in place to differentiate between the adverse events associated with the proposed product and those associated with the reference product, including the identification of adverse events associated with the proposed product that have not been previously associated with the reference product.
cross_references: Sections 505(o)(3) and 505(p)(1)(A)(ii) of the FD&C Act

rules.rule_text: FDA advises sponsors intending to develop biosimilar products to meet with FDA to present their product development plans and establish a schedule of milestones for future discussions with the Agency.
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA advises sponsors intending to develop biosimilar products to meet with FDA to present their product development plans and establish a schedule of milestones that will serve as landmarks for future discussions with the Agency. FDA anticipates that early discussions with FDA about product development plans and about approaches to providing adequate scientific justifications will facilitate biosimilar development.
cross_references: Formal Meetings Between the FDA and Biosimilar Biological Product Sponsors or Applicants

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must consider the complexities of protein products when designing a biosimilar development program, including that even minor structural differences (such as changes in glycosylation patterns) can significantly affect a protein's safety and/or effectiveness.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A, Nature of Protein Products and Related Scientific Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Because even minor structural differences (including certain changes in glycosylation patterns) can significantly affect a protein's safety and/or effectiveness, it is important to evaluate these differences.

rules.rule_text: When the manufacturing process for a marketed protein product is changed, the application holder must assess the effects of the change through appropriate analytical testing, functional assays, and/or animal and/or clinical studies to demonstrate no adverse effect on identity, strength, quality, purity, or potency.
rules.verbatim_quote: Thus, when the manufacturing process for a marketed protein product is changed, the application holder must assess the effects of the change and demonstrate—through appropriate analytical testing, functional assays, and/or in some cases animal and/or clinical studies—that the change does not have an adverse effect on the identity, strength, quality, purity, or potency of the product as they relate to the safety or effectiveness of the product.

rules.rule_text: A sponsor considering manufacturing changes after completing the initial analytical similarity assessment or after completing clinical testing intended to support a 351(k) application must perform an additional analytical similarity assessment with lots manufactured by the new process and establish comparability.
rules.verbatim_quote: A sponsor considering manufacturing changes after completing the initial analytical similarity assessment or after completing clinical testing intended to support a 351(k) application should perform an additional analytical similarity assessment with lots manufactured by the new process and the reference product and establish comparability of the proposed product manufactured by the old and new manufacturing processes.
